title: Scarcity, choice and opportunity cost
summary: This lesson introduces the fundamental economic problem of scarcity, where unlimited wants meet finite resources. It explains how scarcity forces choices and introduces the crucial concept of opportunity cost – the value of the next best alternative forgone. We will also explore the three basic economic questions that every society must answer.
learningObjectives: Define scarcity, choice, and opportunity cost.
Explain the relationship between scarcity and the need to make choices.
Identify the three basic economic questions that arise from scarcity.
Apply the concept of opportunity cost to decisions made by individuals, firms, and governments.

simpleExplanation.title: Why You Can't Have It All
simpleExplanation.summary: Economics starts with a simple truth: we can't have everything we want because our resources are limited. This forces us to make choices, and every choice has a hidden cost.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Imagine you have £10 and you're at a food market. You want both a gourmet burger (£10) and a fancy smoothie (£10), but you can only afford one. Choosing the burger means you give up the smoothie. That forgone smoothie is the 'opportunity cost' of your burger. It's the value of what you didn't choose.
steps: Scarcity forces choice — not all wants can be satisfied.
Opportunity cost is the next best alternative forgone.
What to produce? How? For whom? — basic economic questions.
PPC links scarcity to opportunity cost (see 1.5 for full analysis).

flashcards.back: The fundamental economic problem where unlimited human wants exceed the finite resources available to satisfy them.
The act of selecting among alternatives, which is a necessary consequence of scarcity.
The value of the next best alternative that must be forgone when making an economic decision.
How to allocate scarce resources to best satisfy society's unlimited wants. This is also known as the problem of scarcity.
A good that is not scarce and therefore has a zero opportunity cost in its consumption. For example, air to breathe.
A good that is scarce relative to the demand for it, and thus has an opportunity cost greater than zero. Most goods are economic goods.
One of the three basic economic questions, concerning which goods and services a society should produce with its limited resources.
One of the three basic economic questions, concerning the combination of resources and technology to use in production (e.g., labour-intensive vs. capital-intensive).
One of the three basic economic questions, concerning how the output of goods and services should be distributed among the population.
The scarce resources used to produce goods and services: Land, Labour, Capital, and Entrepreneurship.
A common mistake is to list all forgone alternatives. Opportunity cost is only the value of the *single next best* alternative, not the sum of all of them.
A Latin phrase meaning 'all other things being equal'. It is a key assumption used in economic models to isolate the effect of a single variable.

faq.q: Is money considered a resource in economics?
faq.a: Not directly. Money is a medium of exchange used to acquire resources. The actual economic resources, or 'factors of production', are Land (natural resources), Labour (human effort), Capital (machinery, tools, buildings), and Entrepreneurship. You can't build a factory with a pile of cash; you use the cash to buy the steel, concrete, and labour needed.

faq.q: Can the opportunity cost of a choice ever be zero?
faq.a: Yes, but only for 'free goods' which are not scarce and have no production cost, such as air to breathe or sunlight. For any 'economic good' that requires scarce resources to produce, there will always be an opportunity cost because those resources could have been used to create something else.

faq.q: How is opportunity cost shown on a Production Possibility Curve (PPC)?
faq.a: The slope of the PPC represents the opportunity cost. As you move along the curve to produce more of one good, you must produce less of another. The amount of the other good you give up is the opportunity cost of producing one more unit of the first good. We will explore this graphical representation in detail in topic 1.5 on the PPC.

sections.content: Welcome to the very foundation of economics. At its heart, economics is the study of how people make decisions in a world where they can't have everything they want. This core issue, known as scarcity, shapes the behaviour of individuals, businesses, and entire nations. In this lesson, we'll unpack this concept and explore its immediate consequences: choice and opportunity cost.

Scarcity is the starting point for all economic analysis. It refers to the conflict between human wants, which are infinite, and the resources available to satisfy these wants, which are finite or limited. It's important to distinguish scarcity from poverty. Scarcity affects everyone, from the poorest individual to the wealthiest nation, because no one can have an infinite amount of everything.

The limited resources are called 'factors of production'. Economists categorise them into four groups: Land (all natural resources), Labour (the human effort involved in production), Capital (man-made resources used in production, like machinery and factories), and Entrepreneurship (the skill of organising the other three factors and taking risks).

items: Scarcity exists because wants are unlimited but resources are finite.
Scarcity is a universal problem, affecting all societies and individuals.
The scarce resources are the factors of production: Land, Labour, Capital, and Entrepreneurship.

sections.content: From Scarcity to Choice and Opportunity Cost

Because of scarcity, we cannot satisfy all our wants. This forces us to make choices. When you choose to buy a textbook, you might be choosing not to buy a new video game. When a government chooses to fund a new hospital, it might be choosing not to upgrade a motorway. Every choice involves a trade-off.

This leads us to one of the most important concepts in economics: opportunity cost. The opportunity cost of a decision is the value of the next best alternative that you give up. It's not just about money; it can be time, enjoyment, or any other benefit that you forgo. Understanding opportunity cost is essential for rational decision-making.

sections.question: A student has 4 hours of free time on a Saturday afternoon. They can either study for their economics exam, which they believe will improve their grade from a 5 to a 6, or work a shift at a local café earning £12 per hour. What is the opportunity cost of choosing to study for the 4 hours?
sections.solution:
The opportunity cost is the value of the next best alternative forgone.

1.  **Identify the alternatives:** The student can either (a) study or (b) work.
2.  **Identify the choice:** The student chooses to study.
3.  **Determine the next best alternative:** The next best alternative is working at the café.
4.  **Calculate the value of the forgone alternative:** The value of working is the income that would have been earned. This is 4 hours × £12/hour = £48.

Therefore, the opportunity cost of studying for 4 hours is the £48 the student could have earned.

sections.content: In exams, when asked to define opportunity cost, always use the phrase 'the next best alternative forgone'. Simply saying 'what is given up' is too vague and may not earn full marks. Be precise. For calculation questions, ensure you clearly state what the forgone item is, not just its monetary value.

sections.question: A government has a budget of £500 million for a new national project. It has narrowed the options down to two: building a new high-speed rail link or constructing 50 new schools. After deliberation, the government chooses to build the rail link. What is the opportunity cost of this decision?
sections.solution:
The opportunity cost is the value of the next best alternative that was not chosen.

1.  **Identify the alternatives:** (a) Build a high-speed rail link or (b) construct 50 new schools.
2.  **Identify the choice:** The government chooses the rail link.
3.  **Determine the next best alternative:** The next best alternative is the 50 new schools.

Therefore, the opportunity cost of building the high-speed rail link is the 50 new schools that could have been constructed with the same resources. The opportunity cost is not the £500 million, but what that money could have otherwise been used for in the next best scenario.

sections.content: The problem of scarcity forces every society, regardless of its political or social structure, to answer three fundamental questions about how it will use its resources. The way a society answers these questions defines its economic system (e.g., market economy, command economy, or mixed economy).

items: **What to produce?** This question addresses which goods and services should be produced and in what quantities. Should we produce more consumer goods or more capital goods? More healthcare or more military equipment?

**How to produce?** This question deals with the methods of production. Should we use labour-intensive methods or capital-intensive methods? What is the most efficient way to use our scarce resources?
**For whom to produce?** This question is about distribution. Who gets to consume the goods and services that are produced? Should it be based on income, need, or some other criterion?

sections.label: Test Your Knowledge on Scarcity and Choice
